hurtcore/S337M ITER entry corrected: wrong module (belongs to HURTCORE2) AND fabricated content (described 'S.337-H rash/negligent act' provisions that don't exist anywhere in the extracted data) — replaced with S337M's real four-category Qisas-exclusion structure

hyperlink_passes.status: COMPLETE — FINAL PASS, ALL 9 PASSES COMPLETE

section_subtitle: How the Code connects — 16 relationship types
note: Each entry: label, sub-label, target module key, elementId. Renderer calls startOAt(module, elementId) on tap. All module keys and elementIds verified against ppc_navigator_v82.html.

techniques.title: Prerequisite
techniques.desc: Section A must be established before Section B can fire. Attack prerequisites to collapse dependent charges.

entries.label: General Exceptions (S.76–S.106) prerequisite reading before any specific offence
entries.sub: S.6 makes every offence definition subject to GE by operation of law — GE reads before everything

entries.label: Actus reus + mens rea both prerequisite simultaneously for any criminal offence
entries.sub: S.6 reading rules require both to be established — neither alone is sufficient

entries.label: Stages of crime must proceed in order: intention → preparation → attempt
entries.sub: S.511 attempt requires prior preparation; preparation requires prior intention — sequence is fixed

entries.label: Prior concert (meeting of minds) prerequisite for S.34 Common Intention
entries.sub: Without pre-arranged shared intention, S.34 fails — the most frequently missed prerequisite

entries.label: Abetment mode (S.107) must be identified before S.109–S.120 punishment rules apply
entries.sub: Instigation, Conspiracy, or Aid — establish one mode first; extensions follow the mode

entries.label: S.299 Qatl definitions prerequisite for every S.302–S.338 conviction
entries.sub: The entire Qatl/Qisas framework runs on S.299 vocabulary — the starting point for everything

entries.label: S.305 Wali definition prerequisite for any Qisas claim
entries.sub: Qisas is a personal right of the heirs — establish who the wali is before the right can be exercised

entries.label: S.340 Wrongful Confinement prerequisite for S.343–S.348 aggravated variants
entries.sub: Base confinement must be established before duration or purpose aggravations apply

entries.label: S.378 Theft or S.383 Extortion prerequisite for S.390 Robbery
entries.sub: Robbery cannot be charged unless the underlying theft or extortion is first made out

entries.label: S.415 Cheating definition prerequisite for S.417–S.420 punishment provisions
entries.sub: Punishment sections assume the base definition is made out — always prove S.415 first

entries.label: S.425 Mischief definition prerequisite for S.426–S.440 all variants
entries.sub: Every mischief offence traces back to S.425's foundational definition — start there

entries.label: S.463 Forgery definition prerequisite for S.465–S.477 all aggravated forms
entries.sub: Making a false document (S.464) is the root; every aggravated variant requires it first

entries.label: S.503 Criminal Intimidation definition prerequisite for S.506 punishment
entries.sub: The threat must satisfy S.503's definition before punishment provisions engage

entries.label: S.141 Unlawful Assembly must be proven before S.149 Common Object fires
entries.sub: Defeat the assembly threshold and S.149 collapses entirely — attack headcount or common object

techniques.title: Conjunction
techniques.desc: All elements must be present simultaneously. Unlike Prerequisite (sequential), Conjunction requires A AND B AND C at the same moment.

entries.label: AR ∧ MR ∧ Concurrence — all three required at the same moment for standard liability
entries.sub: Strict Liability is the sole carve-out where MR is dispensed with by deliberate legislative choice

entries.label: S.34: 2+ persons AND prior concert AND act in furtherance — all three at once
entries.sub: Common Intention is a three-element conjunction; any missing element is fatal to the charge

entries.label: S.120-A Conspiracy: agreement AND illegal object — two concurrent requirements
entries.sub: The agreement must be for an illegal purpose; one without the other is insufficient

entries.label: S.107(3) Intentional Aid: intentional facilitation act/omission AND actual assistance to the commission — both required, not either alone
entries.sub: Inadvertent help that happens to assist doesn't count; deliberate help that fails to actually facilitate anything doesn't count either

entries.label: S.300 Murder: actus reus (death caused) AND niyyat (S.300 intention) — concurrent
entries.sub: Both must coexist at the moment of the act; post-hoc intention or accidental death fails

entries.label: S.339 Wrongful Restraint: voluntary obstruction AND the person has a legal right to proceed in that direction — both required
entries.sub: Obstructing someone from going somewhere they had no right to go anyway is not restraint at all

entries.label: S.375 Rape: sexual act AND absence of consent — must coexist at the same moment
entries.sub: Consent given before or after, but not at the time, does not satisfy the conjunction

entries.label: S.378 Theft: Dishonest Intention ∧ Movable Property ∧ Out of Possession ∧ Without Consent ∧ Movement — all five required simultaneously
entries.sub: Failing any single element collapses the whole charge — the Code's own textbook conjunction offence

entries.label: S.390 Robbery: (theft or extortion) AND force/fear AND same transaction
entries.sub: The force must accompany the theft — a gap in time breaks the conjunction

entries.label: S.415 Cheating: deception AND inducement AND resulting delivery or act/omission
entries.sub: The deception must cause the inducement which must cause the consequence — a three-link chain

entries.label: S.465 Forgery: false document AND fraudulent or dishonest intention — both required
entries.sub: A false document without the requisite mental state is not forgery under the section

entries.label: S.499 Defamation: statement AND injury to reputation AND publication to a third party
entries.sub: All three elements must be present — a true statement not published to others is not defamation

entries.label: S.503 Criminal Intimidation: threat AND intention that the victim be alarmed
entries.sub: A threat without the intention to alarm, or alarm without a qualifying threat, fails the section

entries.label: S.141: 5+ persons AND one qualifying common object — both simultaneously
entries.sub: Neither element alone constitutes unlawful assembly; both must coexist

techniques.title: Derivative Liability
techniques.desc: Liability inherited from another section. The accused is guilty of X because they participated in Y — not because they personally did X.

entries.label: S.511 Attempt: punishment derived from the completed offence (half the maximum)
entries.sub: You are sentenced for what you tried to do — the completed offence's regime governs

entries.label: S.34 Common Intention: the act of one is the act of all where prior concert is established
entries.sub: Each co-accused is vicariously liable for every act done in furtherance of the shared intention

entries.label: S.149 Common Object: every assembly member guilty of the offence committed by any member
entries.sub: Personal intention is irrelevant — membership + trigger = derived liability for the group

entries.label: S.120-B(1) Conspiracy: punishment derived from the object offence
entries.sub: Where the object offence carries death/life/2yr+, conspiracy liability mirrors that level

entries.label: S.110–S.114: abettor's punishment derives entirely from the offence abetted
entries.sub: All Schedule II flags — cognizability, bail, compoundability — inherit from the underlying offence

entries.label: S.377B carries no offence definition of its own — its punishment (14–20 years) attaches entirely and derivatively to conduct already defined in S.377A
entries.sub: A punishment-only section: reading S.377B in isolation tells you the sentence but nothing about what conduct triggers it

entries.label: S.396 Murder in Dacoity: all five+ participants liable for killing by any one
entries.sub: You need not personally kill; participation in the dacoity derives the murder liability

entries.label: S.414 Assisting in disposal of stolen property: liability derives from the primary theft
entries.sub: An accessory after the fact — the principal offence determines the exposure

entries.label: S.460 Lurking house-trespass by night: Qatl/hurt by any one = all jointly liable
entries.sub: Group presence at night amplifies derived liability to the most serious outcome

entries.label: S.150 Hiring persons for unlawful assembly: liability flows from the assembly offence
entries.sub: The hirer stands in the shoes of the assembly — Schedule II flags are inherited

techniques.title: Disjunctive Choice
techniques.desc: Any ONE of multiple triggers is sufficient. Defence must defeat ALL limbs. Prosecution need only prove one.

entries.label: General Exceptions: any ONE applicable exception fully negates liability
entries.sub: Prosecution must disprove every raised exception; defence need only establish one

entries.label: Private Defence: body OR property — either right available independently
entries.sub: Both rights operate separately with their own scope and limits under S.100–S.106

entries.label: S.149 Two limbs: 'in prosecution of common object' OR 'members knew likely'
entries.sub: Limb 2 (knew likely) is the broader and more frequently litigated; either alone is sufficient

entries.label: S.120-A Illegal Object: illegal act OR legal act by illegal means OR causing an illegal act OR causing a legal act by illegal means — any ONE suffices
entries.sub: The means, not just the end, can turn an otherwise lawful objective into a criminal conspiracy

entries.label: S.107 Abetment: Instigation OR Conspiracy OR Intentional Aid — any one suffices
entries.sub: Three independent modes; proving any single one makes the accused an abettor

entries.label: S.300 Murder: any ONE of four clauses suffices (intention to kill / GH likely / imminently dangerous etc.)
entries.sub: Four independent routes to qatl-i-amd; each can be charged and proved in the alternative

entries.label: S.304 Proof of Qatl-i-Amd for Qisas: a voluntary confession before a competent court OR evidence under Qanun-e-Shahadat Order Art.17 — either suffices
entries.sub: An evidentiary disjunction, not a substantive one — the same two-mode proof standard also governs hurt liable to Qisas

entries.label: S.350 Criminal Force: force used without consent to commit an offence, OR intending injury/fear/annoyance, OR knowing injury/fear/annoyance is likely — any ONE route suffices
entries.sub: Three independent mental-state routes to the same offence; the prosecution only needs one

entries.label: S.375 Rape: consent vitiated by any ONE of multiple circumstances
entries.sub: Force / fear / intoxication / unsound mind / deception / minority — any one vitiates consent

entries.label: S.390 Robbery: underlying offence is theft OR extortion — disjunctive foundation
entries.sub: Two independent routes to robbery; the charge succeeds if either underlying offence is proved

entries.label: S.415 Cheating: inducing delivery of property OR inducing to do/omit an act
entries.sub: Two independent limbs; property delivery and act/omission are separate routes to the offence

entries.label: S.499 Defamation: any ONE of ten exceptions defeats the charge
entries.sub: Truth for public good / fair comment / judicial proceedings / and seven more — one is enough

entries.label: S.141 Unlawful Assembly: any ONE of five common objects constitutes it
entries.sub: Overawing government / resisting law / mischief / taking property / compelling — one is enough

techniques.title: Branching
techniques.desc: One entry point splits into multiple independent tracks. Each branch has its own rules, elements, and outcomes.

entries.label: General Exceptions: one chapter → twelve independent exception category branches
entries.sub: Mistake / Judicial Acts / Accident / Necessity / Infancy / Insanity / Intoxication / Consent / GF / Trifling / Private Defence

entries.label: Private Defence: one right → two branches (body / property) with independent scope
entries.sub: S.100–S.102 govern the body branch; S.103–S.105 govern property — separate rules apply

entries.label: Punishment (S.53): one framework → five type branches (death / imprisonment / fine / Qisas / Diyat)
entries.sub: Each branch has its own sentencing rules, commutation regime, and court jurisdiction

entries.label: Stages of crime: one act → four stage branches (intention / preparation / attempt / commission)
entries.sub: The law intervenes differently at each stage; the branch determines the charge

entries.label: S.107 Abetment: one entry point → three independent mode branches (Ins / Cons / Aid)
entries.sub: Each mode is a fully independent branch with its own elements — they share only the abetment label

entries.label: S.300 Murder: one section → four clause branches (cl.1 through cl.4)
entries.sub: Four fully independent routes to murder; each branch has its own mental state requirement

entries.label: Qatl: one entry → four independent variant branches (Amd / Shibh-i-Amd / Khata / Bis-Sabab)
entries.sub: Each branch has its own punishment regime, Qisas eligibility, and Diyat calculation

entries.label: S.359 Kidnapping: one concept → two independent branches — S.360 (from Pakistan) and S.361 (from lawful guardianship)
entries.sub: Different actus reus, different victim class, different consent-holder — treat them as two separate offences sharing one name

entries.label: S.499 Defamation: one base offence → ten independent exception branches
entries.sub: Each exception is a fully independent defence with its own elements and burden

entries.label: S.141 Unlawful Assembly: one section → five qualifying common object branches
entries.sub: Each of the five objects is a separate branch with its own factual matrix

entries.label: Blasphemy module: one entry → S.295 (objects) / S.295-B (Quran) / S.295-C (Prophet) branches
entries.sub: Three branches with different actus reus, mens rea, and punishment regimes

techniques.title: Comparative Pair
techniques.desc: Sections that must be understood against each other. Exam questions almost always test your ability to distinguish these.

entries.label: S.82 Infancy (absolute bar, under 10) vs S.83 Infancy (10–14, rebuttable maturity test)
entries.sub: S.82 admits no evidence either way; S.83 turns entirely on proving maturity of understanding on that occasion

entries.label: S.85 Involuntary Intoxication (full defence) vs S.86 Voluntary Intoxication (narrow specific-intent carve-out only)
entries.sub: Voluntary intoxication never negates general-intent liability — only specific-intent offences get the narrower S.86 relief

entries.label: Dishonest vs Fraudulent: the two mental state concepts anchoring all property offences
entries.sub: Dishonest = wrongful gain/loss in property; Fraudulent = deception. Not interchangeable.

entries.label: Literal Rule vs Purposive Approach, applied to S.300: the four murder clauses are joined by ‘or’ and must be read disjunctively
entries.sub: A purposive reading that tried to import extra conditions into any one clause would collapse the section's actual disjunctive structure

entries.label: S.34 Common Intention vs S.149 Common Object — the most-tested pairing in the Code
entries.sub: Headcount / prior concert / scope of liability / evidentiary burden — four structural differences

entries.label: S.34 Common Intention (requires the criminal act to actually be done) vs S.120-A Conspiracy (agreement alone is the completed offence, no act required)
entries.sub: Conspiracy is the earliest possible liability point in group-crime law — CI can't attach until the underlying offence actually happens

entries.label: Instigation vs Conspiracy as abetment modes: active incitement vs passive agreement
entries.sub: Instigation = goading to the specific act; Conspiracy = shared illegal agreement

entries.label: Qisas regime vs Ta'zir regime: private right vs State punishment
entries.sub: Qisas belongs to the wali and can be waived; Ta'zir belongs to the State and cannot

entries.label: Qatl-i-Amd vs Qatl Shibh-i-Amd: intention vs near-intention
entries.sub: Shibh-i-Amd: intends the act but a reasonable person would not expect death — the crucial gap

entries.label: Qatl-i-Amd vs Qatl-i-Khata: intention vs mistake
entries.sub: Amd = intended to cause death; Khata = no intention to harm this particular person at all

entries.label: S.511 general attempt (half the max sentence) vs S.324 Qatl-specific attempt (fixed cap, up to 10 years, minimum 5 for honour-pretext)
entries.sub: S.324 displaces the general STAGES formula entirely for attempted Qatl — it doesn't halve S.302's punishment, it substitutes its own fixed range

entries.label: Isqat-i-Haml with consent vs without consent: same act, opposite moral position
entries.sub: Consent transforms the character of the offence, the punishment, and the Qisas eligibility

entries.label: Isqat-i-Haml (S.338, before organ formation) vs Isqat-i-Janin (S.338B, after organ formation) — same act, the threshold is foetal development, not consent
entries.sub: Two separate comparative axes exist in this cluster: consent (already covered) and developmental stage (this one) — don't conflate them

entries.label: Wrongful Restraint (S.339) vs Wrongful Confinement (S.340): partial vs total restriction
entries.sub: Restraint prevents movement in a direction; confinement restricts movement in all directions

entries.label: S.377 (1860, penetration required, any victim) vs S.377A (2016, no penetration required, victim under 18 only) — two different offences, not overlapping degrees of the same one
entries.sub: S.377A doesn't replace or narrow S.377 — it fills a gap S.377's penetration requirement left completely uncovered

entries.label: Robbery vs Dacoity: five-person threshold changes the entire offence category
entries.sub: Dacoity = robbery by five or more; the headcount transforms punishment and cognizability

entries.label: Criminal Breach of Trust (S.405) vs Theft (S.378): lawful possession vs unlawful taking
entries.sub: CBT starts with lawful custody; theft starts with taking what was never in your custody

entries.label: S.428 (general animals, 10rs+ value) vs S.429 (named livestock at ANY value, or other animals at 50rs+)
entries.sub: Named livestock — elephants, camels, horses, cattle — get value-blind protection; everything else needs to clear a rupee threshold first

entries.label: Forgery (S.463) vs Cheating (S.415): document-based vs person-based deception
entries.sub: Forgery operates through false documents; cheating operates directly on a person's mind

entries.label: S.499 Defamation: truth alone vs truth for public good — not the same defence
entries.sub: Truth without public good purpose does not satisfy Exception 1; a critical distinction

entries.label: Criminal Intimidation (S.503) vs Extortion (S.383): threat vs threat to extract property
entries.sub: Extortion uses threat specifically to extract property; intimidation's object is broader

entries.label: S.121 Waging War: wages war, attempts to wage war, OR abets the waging of war — all three carry the IDENTICAL punishment ceiling (death or life)
entries.sub: A deliberate departure from the general STAGES principle that attempt draws only half the maximum sentence — for this one offence, intention-in-motion is treated as gravely as completion

entries.label: S.128 voluntary escape of a State prisoner (life/10yr) vs S.129 negligent escape of the same (3yr, simple imprisonment) — identical outcome, mens rea is the entire distinction
entries.sub: Same custodian, same prisoner, same escape — the sentencing gap runs entirely on intent versus carelessness

techniques.title: Cascade / Waterfall
techniques.desc: A fires, which automatically produces B as a legal consequence, which then produces C. Not a gate you choose to pass — a chain that fires automatically.

entries.label: Attempt fails → S.511 automatically triggers → half-sentence from the target offence cascades
entries.sub: No separate charge needed; S.511 cascades from the incomplete attempt automatically

entries.label: Unlawful assembly formed → offence committed in prosecution → S.149 liability fires for all members
entries.sub: Every member is swept in automatically once the assembly and disjunctive trigger are established

entries.label: Criminal Conspiracy formed → object offence committed → S.120-B punishment cascades from that offence
entries.sub: The conspiracy liability level is automatically determined by the object offence's punishment

entries.label: Abetment mode established → S.109 punishment attaches → extension rules S.110–S.120 fire
entries.sub: Establishing any mode triggers the extension chain automatically based on what happens next

entries.label: Qatl-i-Amd → Qisas right vests → Wali decides → S.311 State override may apply
entries.sub: Four automatic steps; each triggered by the outcome of the previous — a complete cascade

entries.label: Qisas waived by wali → S.311 Fasad-fil-Arz may activate → State imprisonment cascades in
entries.sub: The waiver triggers a secondary regime automatically — Fasad-fil-Arz is the cascade response to waiver

entries.label: S.328 Exposure of a child → if the child dies as a result, a separate and more serious Qatl-i-Amd / Shibh-i-Amd / Bis-Sabab charge attaches
entries.sub: The module's own text is explicit: S.328 liability does not displace the homicide charge — both can run

entries.label: S.383 Extortion: fear induced → dishonest inducement operates on the victim's will → property delivered as a result — a causal chain, not simultaneous elements
entries.sub: Unlike Theft's conjunction, Extortion's elements only work in sequence: no fear, no inducement; no inducement, no delivery

entries.label: Theft + force at same time → robbery triggered → if five or more → dacoity automatically
entries.sub: Two cascades in sequence: theft+force=robbery is automatic; robbery×5 persons=dacoity is automatic

entries.label: Dacoity committed → murder occurs during it → S.396 liability waterfalls to all five+ participants
entries.sub: The dacoity activates a cascade that makes all participants liable for any killing by any one

techniques.title: Parent-Child Hierarchy
techniques.desc: One section defines the concept (Parent); multiple sections elaborate variants (Children). Children inherit the parent's vocabulary and cannot exist without it.

entries.label: S.53 Tripartite Framework (Parent) → S.54–55 Commutation / S.60–70 Imprisonment Mechanics / S.71–72 Doubtful-Case Limits / S.73–74 Solitary Limits / S.75 Recidivism (Children)
entries.sub: Six implementing sections all inherit and elaborate S.53's five punishment types; none stand alone

entries.label: S.107 Abetment modes (Parent) → S.108A, S.109–S.120 all extensions (Children)
entries.sub: The three modes define what abetment is; all extensions below them depend on that foundation

entries.label: S.299 (Parent) → S.302 Amd / S.316 Shibh-i-Amd / S.319 Khata / S.322 Bis-Sabab (Children)
entries.sub: Four qatl varieties share one definitional parent; all children inherit S.299 vocabulary

entries.label: S.332 (Parent, five-way classification) → Itlaf-i-Udw (S.333-334) / Itlaf-i-Salahiyyat-i-Udw (S.335-336) / Shajjah (S.337) / Jurh (S.337B-D) / all other hurt (Children)
entries.sub: S.332(2)'s own five-way classification is the map every subsequent Hurt section fills in — nothing in this cluster stands outside it

entries.label: S.339 Wrongful Restraint (Parent) → S.341–S.348 punishment variants (Children)
entries.sub: Base restraint and confinement are the parents; aggravated duration and purpose are children

entries.label: S.349 Force (Grandparent) → S.350 Criminal Force (Parent) → S.351 Assault (Child)
entries.sub: A three-generation hierarchy: each level adds one element to the previous definition

entries.label: S.383 Extortion (Parent) → S.384–S.389 all variants and aggravated forms (Children)
entries.sub: Six children elaborate the base extortion definition with escalating fear and threat scenarios

entries.label: S.391 Dacoity (Parent) → S.395–S.402 all punishment and variant children
entries.sub: Eight children depend on S.391's definition; none can be charged without the parent being made out

entries.label: S.378 Theft (Parent) → S.379 punishment / S.380 in building / S.381 by servant / S.382 with preparation
entries.sub: Four children each add one aggravating circumstance to the parent theft definition

entries.label: S.415 Cheating (Parent) → S.417 (personation) / S.418 (duty) / S.419 / S.420 (inducing delivery)
entries.sub: Four children each specifying a variant; all inherit the deception element from S.415

entries.label: S.425 Mischief (Parent) → S.426–S.440 all fifteen mischief variants (Children)
entries.sub: Fifteen children all elaborate S.425's foundational definition in different factual matrices

entries.label: S.463 Forgery (Parent) → S.465–S.477-A all aggravated forms (Children)
entries.sub: Every forgery offence inherits from S.464's false document test — the root of the family tree

entries.label: S.141 Unlawful Assembly (Parent) → S.142–S.160 all public tranquillity children
entries.sub: Every offence in the Public Tranquillity chapter depends on S.141's foundational definition

techniques.title: Nesting
techniques.desc: Definitions within definitions within definitions. Vertical depth — to understand A you must first unwrap B, which requires C, which requires D.

entries.label: GE S.84 Unsound Mind: 'unsound mind' imports psychiatric concepts not defined in the Code
entries.sub: The deepest nest in the Code: the definition points outside the statute entirely

entries.label: S.6 Reading Rules: the topmost shell — every offence nests within S.6's framework
entries.sub: S.6 is the outer wrapper that holds everything; it is the parent nest of the entire Code

entries.label: S.34 CI: 'common intention' → S.39 voluntarily → S.23 gain/loss — definitions nest downward
entries.sub: Three words in S.34 import a chain of definitions from the General Explanations chapter

entries.label: S.149 Common Object: S.149 → S.141 → five common objects → each defined elsewhere
entries.sub: S.149 is a two-line section that imports two full definitional layers beneath it

entries.label: S.300 Murder: S.300 → S.299 → S.39 voluntarily → S.23 gain/loss — four levels deep
entries.sub: Each level unwraps a further definition; the deepest nesting in the property-adjacent chain

entries.label: Force (S.349, physical definition) → Criminal Force (S.350, Force + mens rea) → Assault (S.351, mere apprehension of Criminal Force — no contact needed at all)
entries.sub: Each definition presupposes the one before it; Assault is the furthest-nested and requires no physical contact whatsoever

entries.label: S.390 Robbery → S.378 Theft → 'dishonestly' → S.24 → S.23 — five levels deep
entries.sub: Robbery is the shallowest entry; the deepest nest to understand it runs five definitions down

entries.label: S.378 Theft: 'dishonestly' → S.24 → S.23 gain/loss — three definitional levels deep
entries.sub: One word in the theft section imports a stack of definitions below the surface

entries.label: S.463 Forgery: 'false document' → S.464 definition → 'fraudulently' → S.25 — nested layers
entries.sub: The forgery section's key terms each unpack into further definitions in adjacent sections

techniques.title: Escalation Ladder
techniques.desc: Sections arranged in ascending severity. Each rung adds exactly one aggravating circumstance to the rung below. Knowing the ladder tells you where any fact pattern sits.

entries.label: Stages ladder: St1 Intention (unpunishable) → St2 Preparation (punishable only in named exceptions) → St3 Attempt (S.511, half sentence) → St4 Completion (full sentence)
entries.sub: Each rung is a step up in both proximity to harm and the punishment a court may impose

entries.label: Abetment ladder: S.116 (imprisonment offence not committed) → S.115 (death/life offence not committed) → S.118 (offence committed)
entries.sub: The gravity of the abetted offence and whether it was committed are the two rungs

entries.label: Shajjah ladder: Khafifah → Mudihah → Hashimah → Munaqqilah → Ammah → Damighah — six rungs of head/face wound severity
entries.sub: The module's own text calls these 'six progressively severe sub-types' — Diyat percentage and Ta'zir ceiling both climb with each rung

entries.label: Wrongful Confinement ladder: S.342 → S.343 (3+ days) → S.344 (10+ days) → S.347 (to extort) → S.348 (to confess)
entries.sub: Five rungs; each rung adds one aggravating element (duration, purpose) to the previous

entries.label: Assault ladder: S.352 (base) → S.353 (vs public servant) → S.354 (vs woman modesty) → S.354-A (stripping)
entries.sub: Victim identity and degree of violation determine the rung; each adds punishment

entries.label: Kidnapping ladder: S.363 (base) → S.364 (to murder) → S.365-A (extortion) → S.364-A (under-14 victim)
entries.sub: Purpose and victim vulnerability are the rungs; each multiplies the exposure

entries.label: Theft punishment ladder: S.379 base (3yr) → S.380 dwelling house (7yr) → S.381/381A clerk-servant or motor vehicle (7yr) → S.382 with violence-preparation (10yr, rigorous)
entries.sub: S.382 sits at what the module's own text calls 'the conceptual threshold between pure Theft and Robbery'

entries.label: Extortion ladder: S.384 (base) → S.386 (fear of death/GH) → S.388 (accusation of serious offence)
entries.sub: The nature of the threat escalates; each rung increases the fear threshold required

entries.label: Robbery/Dacoity ladder: S.392 (robbery) → S.395 (dacoity) → S.397 (deadly weapon) → S.396 (murder in dacoity)
entries.sub: Headcount, then armament, then lethality — three aggravating rungs above the base

entries.label: Receiving ladder: S.411 base (3yr) → S.412 dacoity-tainted property (life) → S.413 habitual dealing (life/10yr)
entries.sub: S.412's jump to life imprisonment specifically traces to the property's Dacoity origin — the same organised-crime concern already visible throughout the Robbery module

entries.label: Mischief ladder: S.426 (base) → S.427 (Rs.50+ damage) → S.435 (by fire) → S.436 (intent to destroy house)
entries.sub: Value of damage, then method, then target escalate across the chapter

entries.label: House trespass ladder: S.447 → S.448 (house) → S.449/450 (for capital/life offence) → S.452 (after preparation for hurt)
entries.sub: Location, then purpose, then preparation for violence escalate the charge

entries.label: Lurking trespass ladder: S.453 (lurking) → S.456 (by night) → S.459 (hurt during) → S.460 (qatl during)
entries.sub: Concealment, then timing, then harm escalate to the maximum lurking trespass exposure

entries.label: Forgery ladder: S.465 (base) → S.466 (court records) → S.467 (valuable security) → S.468 (for cheating)
entries.sub: The target of the forgery and its purpose determine the rung

entries.label: S.494 base bigamy vs S.495 bigamy with concealment from the new spouse — concealment is the aggravating fact that escalates punishment
entries.sub: The Exception's own disclosure condition is exactly what S.495 turns into a separate, harsher offence

entries.label: Unlawful assembly punishment: S.143 mere membership (6mo) → S.144 armed with a deadly weapon (2yr) → S.145 defying a lawful dispersal order (2yr)
entries.sub: The module's own text calls these 'Base, Armed, and Defiance Tiers' — each rung adds one aggravating fact to simple membership

techniques.title: Iceberg
techniques.desc: Small visible statutory text; enormous hidden content beneath. The most important provisions in the Code are often the shortest on the surface.

entries.label: S.99 Private Defence limits: four limitations are beneath every private defence claim
entries.sub: S.96 gives the right; S.99 is the iceberg that qualifies it — most litigated, least visible

entries.label: S.6: two lines of text that silently import the entire General Exceptions chapter (30 sections)
entries.sub: The smallest section with the largest mass — every offence is subject to S.76–106 without being told so

entries.label: 'Public servant' in any offence: S.21's elaborate 12-category definition beneath every mention
entries.sub: Every time 'public servant' appears, S.21's twelve categories travel with it invisibly

entries.label: 'Dishonestly' in S.378 Theft: one word importing S.24 → S.23 and the entire property mental element framework
entries.sub: The mass beneath this single word is larger than most full sections in the Code

entries.label: 'Good faith' in GE exceptions: S.52's definition beneath every General Exception that uses the phrase
entries.sub: Seven GE exceptions import S.52 without saying so; good faith jurisprudence is the hidden mass

entries.label: S.511 Attempt: one section that implicitly references every imprisonable offence in the Code
entries.sub: The smallest provision with the widest reach — it applies across the entire Code silently

entries.label: S.34 'common intention': three words importing the entire prior-concert jurisprudence
entries.sub: Mahbub Shah 1945 and generations of case law are beneath those three words

entries.label: S.149: two sentences importing S.141's entire five-object framework and decades of case law
entries.sub: The tip is visible; the mass is unlawful assembly doctrine, headcount disputes, and common object jurisprudence

entries.label: S.107 'abets': the entire abetment architecture hidden beneath one five-letter word
entries.sub: One word used throughout the Code; every use carries the three-mode definition invisibly

techniques.title: Layering
techniques.desc: Multiple legal frameworks applying simultaneously to the same act. Unlike Islamization Overlay (a historical event), Layering is the general structural technique.

entries.label: S.5 Savings Clause: PPC is one layer among several — Army Act, Anti-Terrorism Act 1997, and narcotics law all apply simultaneously
entries.sub: S.5 is the Code's own admission that it doesn't occupy the field alone — check the special-law layer before assuming PPC governs

entries.label: S.34 CI and S.149 CO: both can be charged simultaneously against the same accused on same facts
entries.sub: Prosecution layers two constructive liability frameworks; defence must defeat both

entries.label: S.109 Abetment and S.120-B Conspiracy: both frameworks can apply to the same conduct simultaneously
entries.sub: An accused who abetted through a conspiracy faces both layers independently

entries.label: Anti-Terrorism Act + PPC: ATA adds a terror-schedule layer on top of any underlying PPC offence
entries.sub: The same murder can be simultaneously a PPC S.302 and an ATA offence — two court jurisdictions may apply

entries.label: Qatl-i-Amd: Qisas layer (private right of heirs) + Ta'zir layer (State power) simultaneously
entries.sub: Two complete punishment regimes on one conviction; the wali's decision determines which activates

entries.label: S.311 Fasad-fil-Arz: PPC private law layer + Islamic public interest doctrine layer on same case
entries.sub: The private settlement layer (Qisas waived) and the public law layer (State override) coexist

entries.label: Diyat compensation layer + Ta'zir imprisonment layer can both attach to the same Qatl-i-Khata
entries.sub: Civil compensation (Diyat) and State punishment (Ta'zir) are independent layers on one negligent act

entries.label: PPC + PECA 2016: two statutory layers applying simultaneously to the same digital act
entries.sub: Cybercrime carries the PPC base layer (cheating, forgery, defamation) plus PECA's enhanced regime

entries.label: S.459 Hurt/Qatl during trespass: trespass punishment AND ALSO the full Chapter XVI Qatl/hurt punishment — cumulative, not merged into one sentence
entries.sub: Two convictions running together for one course of conduct — the trespass charge isn't subsumed into or discounted by the homicide charge

entries.label: Blasphemy S.295-C: PPC criminal layer + Constitutional Article 19/20 rights layer in tension
entries.sub: The criminal prohibition and the constitutional right to religion operate simultaneously

techniques.title: Islamization Overlay
techniques.desc: Where 1979–1990 changed what 1860 built. The Code's original common-law skeleton carries Islamic-law grafts. This map shows every seam.

entries.label: S.53 Punishment list: Qisas / Diyat / Arsh / Daman added alongside Death and Imprisonment
entries.sub: The most visible seam — two vocabularies from two legal universes now share one list

entries.label: S.53 seam in full: two lineages share one punishment list — the most visible structural overlay
entries.sub: Read alongside Rabbit Hole essays RH1 (Two Lineages) and RH10 (The Silence in the Law)

entries.label: The Silence broken: Macaulay's deliberate moral neutrality ended by Zia's amendments
entries.sub: The Code never said why acts are wrong — 1979 grafted Islamic moral justification onto specific chapters

entries.label: S.299–S.338H: entire Qatl and Hurt framework replaced the 1860 provisions from scratch in 1990
entries.sub: The Qisas and Diyat Ordinance deleted and substituted the entire Chapter XVI

entries.label: Wali concept: right of Qisas held by heirs of victim — not the State
entries.sub: 1860's State-as-prosecutor model replaced by a private family right; a fundamental architectural shift

entries.label: S.311 Fasad-fil-Arz: State override mechanism inserted to re-capture cases heirs might waive
entries.sub: A patch on the Qisas architecture — the State can still punish even if the wali forgives

entries.label: Hurt chapter: Shajjah / Jurh / Jaifah / Arsh vocabulary inserted 1990
entries.sub: Classical Arabic wound taxonomy now governs injury law where English hurt categories once did

entries.label: Isqat-i-Haml and Isqat-i-Janin: Islamic miscarriage provisions replaced 1860 original
entries.sub: Organ formation (120 days) as the legal threshold — a classical fiqh concept, not common law

entries.label: S.498A Fornication: inserted 1979 as part of Hudood Ordinances
entries.sub: The 1860 S.497 Adultery was a different offence; S.498A added an entirely new category

entries.label: S.295-B and S.295-C Blasphemy: inserted by Zia in 1982 and 1986 — not in the 1860 Code
entries.sub: The original S.295 protected all religions equally; these provisions asymmetrically protect one

techniques.title: Cross-Chapter Arc
techniques.desc: A concept threading across multiple chapters. Follow the arc to see how one idea transforms as it moves through the Code.

entries.label: Consent arc: GE S.87–S.92 → S.375 Rape → S.493A–S.498C Marriage offences
entries.sub: Consent vitiates or enables; its definition shifts depending on the chapter context

entries.label: Public servant arc: S.21 definition → S.121–S.130 State offences → S.409 CBT by public servant
entries.sub: The public servant's special status creates heightened liability across three separate chapters

entries.label: Mens rea arc: S.39 voluntarily → S.24/25 dishonestly/fraudulently → S.299 niyyat → S.415 deception
entries.sub: The mental element vocabulary from General Explanations threads through every major offence chapter

entries.label: Good faith arc: S.52 defined in GEXPL → S.88–S.95 GE exceptions → Defamation exceptions
entries.sub: Good faith is a universal gatekeeper that threads through exceptions across three chapters

entries.label: Stages arc: Intention → Preparation (S.399) → Attempt (S.511) → Commission (across all chapters)
entries.sub: The law's intervention points trace an arc from mental state to completed harm

entries.label: Reform arc: Theories of Punishment → Punishment chapter → Qatl framework → Islamization essays
entries.sub: How the 1860 punishment architecture was overlaid by the 1979–1990 Islamization wave

entries.label: Group liability arc: S.34 CI (2+) → S.120-A Conspiracy (2+) → S.141 Assembly (5+) → S.149 CO (5+)
entries.sub: Four group liability provisions in ascending headcount and decreasing prior-concert requirement

entries.label: Abetment arc: S.107 modes → S.108A jurisdiction → S.109–S.120 punishment extensions
entries.sub: Abetment begins with three modes and fans out into eighteen distinct punishment scenarios

entries.label: Harm escalation arc: Restraint → Force/Assault → Hurt → Qatl (four ascending chapters)
entries.sub: Each chapter builds on the harm concept of the one before; the arc traces the law of violence

entries.label: Property dishonesty arc: Misappropriation → Receiving → Cheating → Fraudulent Deeds
entries.sub: Four chapters covering the full spectrum of property offences rooted in dishonesty

entries.label: Document integrity arc: Forgery → Trade Marks → Currency Notes (three chapters ascending)
entries.sub: Three chapters on document/mark falsification in ascending institutional importance

entries.label: Reverse-burden-of-proof arc: TRADEMARKS S.482 ('unless they prove no intent to defraud') → S.486 ('unless they prove reasonable precautions') → CURRENCY S.489F ('burden of proof resting on' the accused)
entries.sub: A recurring drafting technique across the property-mark and currency chapters — the offence is presumed on the facts, and the accused must displace it

entries.label: CURRENCY's counterfeit/use/possess-materials triad (S.489A / S.489B_C / S.489D) mirrors FORGERY's own making/using/possessing structure applied to a different instrument class
entries.sub: Same three-stage offence architecture, same escalating culpability logic — learn the shape once at Forgery, recognise it again at Currency

techniques.title: Iterations
techniques.desc: The same legal structure repeated with incremental variations. Find the variable that changes between iterations and you have found the decision point.

entries.label: S.87 → S.88 → S.89 → S.90 → S.91 → S.92: six consent variations, same underlying structure
entries.sub: The variable across each: harm severity, who can consent, what vitiates consent, and what falls outside consent's reach entirely

entries.label: S.115(1) and S.115(2): same abetment structure — variable is whether harm actually resulted
entries.sub: Two rows, one structure; the variable is the downstream consequence of the abetted act

entries.label: S.116(1) and S.116(2): same non-commission structure — variable is whether abettor is a public servant
entries.sub: The public servant iteration always carries a higher sentence; one variable, one rung up

entries.label: S.337-A(i)–(vi): Shajjah wound classification — six iterations of the same structure
entries.sub: Depth of penetration and structural damage are the variables across six head/face wound rungs

entries.label: S.337-F(i)–(vi): Jurh body wound classification — six iterations of the same structure
entries.sub: The same wound classification logic iterated six times for body wounds below the neck

entries.label: S.337M's four Qisas-exclusion categories: minor/insane offender, victim's own request, victim's pre-existing organ imperfection, offender's matching organ missing
entries.sub: Same 'not liable to Qisas' structure, four different disqualifying facts — compare against S.306's Qatl exclusions from Pass 3

entries.label: Arsh scales by organ multiplicity: single organs (S.337Q) = full Diyat, paired organs (S.337R) = half Diyat per organ, quadruplicate organs (S.337S) = quarter Diyat per organ
entries.sub: The fraction is always 1/n, where n is how many of that organ type the body normally has — same formula, three iterations

entries.label: S.388 and S.388(2): extortion by accusation iterated — variable is nature of the offence threatened
entries.sub: Same threat structure; the variable is whether the threatened accusation is an unnatural offence

entries.label: S.421 → S.422 → S.423 → S.424: same dishonest-or-fraudulent-defeat-of-creditors structure, four different methods
entries.sub: The variable across each: physical removal, debt-blocking, false-consideration deeds, claim-release — same mens rea and target, different mechanics

entries.label: S.482 and S.486: same reverse-burden structure, different things the accused must prove — absence of fraudulent intent vs reasonable precautions taken
entries.sub: Both shift the proof burden onto the defendant once the prosecution shows the mark or goods were false — the variable is only what discharges it

entries.label: S.498A → S.498B → S.498C: same 2011/2017-era women's-protection structure, three different harms — inheritance deprivation, forced marriage, Quran-marriage
entries.sub: All three share the same drafting DNA: modern amendments closing gaps the 1860 Code never anticipated

entries.label: S.499 Exceptions 1–10: same exception structure iterated ten times — variable is the justification
entries.sub: Ten rows, one logical template; find which exception applies by finding which variable fits

entries.label: S.506(1) and S.506(2): criminal intimidation iterated — variable is gravity of the threat
entries.sub: Base intimidation vs threat of death/grievous hurt; one variable separates the two rows

entries.label: S.133 (mere abetment of assault on a superior officer, 3yr) → S.134 (same abetment where the assault actually occurs, 7yr) — the identical 'variable = whether the abetted act happened' structure already seen in Abetment's S.115/S.116
entries.sub: Same drafting pattern, different chapter — once learned in the general Abetment module, it's instantly recognisable here

techniques.title: Structural Cross-Reference
techniques.desc: Sections explicitly defined in terms of another section in the statute. Section B cannot be read without Section A.

entries.label: Private defence limits (S.99) explicitly cross-reference S.100–S.106 for scope and occasions
entries.sub: S.99 states what you cannot do; S.100–106 state when you can — they must be read as one block

entries.label: S.6 reading rules cross-reference all General Exceptions as silently incorporated in every offence
entries.sub: Every offence definition is automatically subject to S.76–106 by virtue of S.6 alone — universal cross-ref

entries.label: S.511 Attempt: carries no punishment of its own — always refers to another section for the sentence
entries.sub: Every S.511 charge explicitly cross-references the completed offence to calculate the sentence

entries.label: S.149 explicitly names S.141: the section imports unlawful assembly by direct statutory reference
entries.sub: S.149 is two sentences built entirely on S.141 — the cross-reference is on the face of the text

entries.label: S.149 withdrawal standard explicitly mirrors S.34's: objective disassociation before the offence occurs, in both sections
entries.sub: Learn the withdrawal test once at Common Intention, apply it identically at Common Object — no separate doctrine to memorise

entries.label: S.120-A Criminal Conspiracy cross-references S.43 (illegal act) in the agreement element
entries.sub: The object of the conspiracy must be 'illegal' as defined in S.43 — a direct textual bridge

entries.label: S.108A Abetment jurisdiction cross-references S.107 as its operating condition
entries.sub: S.108A extends territorial jurisdiction but only where S.107 abetment is already established

entries.label: S.108A Abetment jurisdiction gap-filler cross-references the general extraterritorial framework in JUR S.3–S.4
entries.sub: S.108A is not a new principle — it's S.3/S.4's extraterritorial logic applied specifically to abettors who stay physically inside Pakistan

entries.label: S.300 Murder refers back to S.299 Qatl definitions throughout its four clauses explicitly
entries.sub: S.300 cannot be read without S.299's vocabulary — the cross-reference is structural, not implied

entries.label: S.311 Fasad-fil-Arz explicitly cross-references S.302 as its operating condition
entries.sub: S.311 only operates where S.302 has been established and then waived — the cross-reference is the trigger

entries.label: S.338 Isqat-i-Haml cross-references S.338A–C for all its sub-definitions explicitly
entries.sub: S.338 names and incorporates S.338A-C; all four sections form one inseparable cross-referenced unit

entries.label: S.382 Theft-with-violence-preparation explicitly borders Robbery's T∨E element — the same advance-preparation-for-violence fact pattern that would make it Robbery if the force were actually used
entries.sub: Read S.382 and Robbery's opening element side by side: the only thing missing from a full Robbery charge is the force itself being deployed

entries.label: S.390 Robbery explicitly defined in terms of S.378 Theft and S.383 Extortion
entries.sub: Robbery is statutory shorthand for (theft or extortion) + force/fear; neither root can be read alone

entries.label: S.391 Dacoity explicitly cross-references S.390 Robbery as its foundation
entries.sub: Dacoity is defined as robbery committed by five or more — S.390 is the explicit baseline

entries.label: S.411's mens rea — 'knowing or having reason to believe' — imports GEXPL's S.26 objective evidentiary standard directly
entries.sub: Reason to believe is judged objectively, not by what the accused actually thought — same standard as GE's own Reason to Believe entry

entries.label: S.489B_C's mens rea — 'knowing or having reason to believe' — imports GEXPL's S.26 objective evidentiary standard directly
entries.sub: Third module this project has traced back to the same GEXPL gate — Theft, Receiving, and now Currency all run on the identical objective test
